metadata.reasoning_chain:
The self-inductance is related to the energy as
\[
W=\frac{L I^{2}}{2}
\]

Let us calculate the energy of the configuration
\[
W=\frac{1}{2 \mu_{o}} \int_{V} B^{2} \mathrm{~d} \tau
\]

Next, we need to find the magnetic field. From Ampère's law, the only significant (non-zero magnetic field region) is for radius $s$ between $a$ and $b$.
\[
\oint \vec{B} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \vec{\ell}=\mu_{o} I_{\mathrm{enc}}
\]
where $I_{\text {enc }}=I$ is the enclosed current. The left-hand side is given by
\[
\oint \vec{B} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \vec{\ell}=B 2 \pi s
\]

From here,
\[
\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi s} \hat{\phi}
\]

The energy of a cylindrical shell of length $\ell$, radius $s$ and thickness $\mathrm{d} s$ is:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{d} W & =\frac{1}{2 \mu_{o}} B^{2} 2 \pi \ell s \mathrm{~d} s \\
\mathrm{~d} W & =\frac{1}{2 \mu_{o}}\left(\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi s}\right)^{2} 2 \pi \ell s \mathrm{~d} s \\
\mathrm{~d} W & =\frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{~d} s}{s}
\end{aligned}
\]

We obtain the total energy by integrating over $s$ from $a$ to $b$
\[
W=\int_{a}^{b} \frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{~d} s}{s}=\left.\frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \ln s\right|_{a} ^{b}=\frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \ln \frac{b}{a}
\]

Back to impedance per unit length:
\[
W=\frac{L I^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{L}{\ell}=\frac{2 W}{I^{2} \ell}=\frac{\mu_{o}}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{b}{a}
\]
